speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. The date is May 31, 1996. And we're interviewing Mr. Sol Glick today. So tell me. Why wouldn't your father let you go to Israel, to Palestine at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he don't want me-- he wants the children to be with him. He was very-- he was afraid something going to happen. You're a young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come to him and say, Dad, I'd like to go with you-- I'd like to go with my friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never even asked him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I said, I'm registered. I'm going to Israel. He said, what? Over my dead body.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you stay with us, with your brothers. You're not going away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so that-- that-- and then right thereafter, you went into the army, and then you came back. And then what happened in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, I was working-- before 1939, maybe a week or two-- I was working-- we know it's gonna be a war. But we didn't expect that Hitler is going to attack Poland. He has [? caught ?] Czechoslovakia. He went through there and everything. My father used to go in the morning. He used to go down 2 o'clock, 3 o'clock, taking care if people come, and feed the cows and everything. And he didn't know how to read. It was already-- [NON-ENGLISH]. They mobilized the people to come register. Hitler attacked Poland. And the bombs was already falling. And I got dressed. And I went down. And I said to-- to my brothers, look, they're calling my-- my age, my younger brother's-- my older brother's age. They want you to register to go-- for me to go in the army. Hitler attacked Poland. When we went there to register, there was nobody to talk to already. Everything was in a shamble, nobody to talk. We come back--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was that after what? A day?

speaker: interviewee
text: A day two days later it, it was already-- he-- he-- like a blitzkrieg. He bombed every city. And we decide-- and we decide to walk to Warsaw. Warsaw was still fighting. They will be protected and everything. And as we were walking, the jets, airplanes, landed on the-- on the highway, and shooting people like crazy. We were running through the woods, first. It's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you going with? Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we're a whole group. We were all running, young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your brothers? How many brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four. All of--

speaker: interviewer
text: All five of you were together?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father was home with my mother. My younger brother was in the army. He was already drafted. But we were going, the whole city running. The bombs-- the bombs was falling there and everything. Warsaw was like a castle. Every house is like, you cannot get in. It was a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I remember-- I got the news that my brother got wounded, when I was in Warsaw, the younger brother. So who is gonna go? They send me to go to the hospital and see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was going from one building to another one, the bombs was silent. And it took me a long time to get out from the house, and the bombs was calm. And I got to the hospital, he was wounded. He was with bandages. And I came in, they bombed the hospital. And I grabbed him, and went down to the cellar. And I got him home. And then-- and we decide what we gonna do. We don't know what's happening. After--

speaker: interviewer
text: You got him home back to Blonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, where were you staying in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we stayed-- any-- any-- any apartment, you could take it. People-- it was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just walked in somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked into a-- and we-- we stayed not in apartment. We stayed in hallways and everything, afraid. Later on, when Hitler announced he's gonna take away-- he took away the water. He took away the food. He bombed Warsaw. And he said to the mayor, from the city who was in charge of the army who was fighting, and we-- and I saw he-- he dropped leaflets. And it said, if you don't surrender, we're gonna destroy the whole city. And the mayor surrendered. Just in the history, you should mention when he came to sign the peace, he shot him. He killed the-- he killed the mayor surrendering for peace. He was-- just the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later on, Warsaw was finished. When I-- when I-- and when Russia attacked, the whole army was still on the other side. You know, and the army was taken by the Russians, the whole army. And they send them-- they were allies. And they send them to prison. And we said, what's this? We're not gonna stay here. Somebody came from Bialystok, the city-- So, how long was that? How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: About two, three, or four days later, the war was over. And our friends come from Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: The far wasn't over, just the battle--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Poland was over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was over, Russia took a half, and Germany took a half. Now men came, and my cousin came from Bialystok. And he said--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him? How did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he came back to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but if were-- if you were hiding out-- if you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, were were not hiding after this. The war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you-- then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were walking free. And he said, you better come to Bialystok, across-- across the border. Here is not gonna be good. And the old Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: So then Bialystok by that time was in the Russian sector?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Russia took Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Bialystok is about how many miles north of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about 50 kilometers, 100 kilometers from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: North of Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he said, the old Germans came in, the old Germans. After-- after the war was over, we walked back to the city of Blonie, and went back to the city. And I saw a monstrosity. People were going home, and the-- and the Germans round them up for work, for everything. It was terrible. And the people, the Hasidim there was in the street, sweeping the floors, with the-- with the [? shawls. ?] And they were shouting at people like crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they took him out, and they cut the [? payot ?] and make him work. They make him into a circus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, people that you knew, that you grew up with?

words.text: you knew, that you grew up with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know them. They were going back to the cities, and small towns, the [NON-ENGLISH]. The war was over-- to see the parents, and everything. It was terrible. And-- and-- and two Germans who was-- well, they came from the First World War. You see, when Hitler occupied our city, the old Germans who was in the city before who occupied Poland-- Poland was occupied by Germany and Russia. And they say to us--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about during World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But the ones come back after Hitler took our city, the people who was in Blonie, Warsaw--

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew the old Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The old Germans come back that know everybody. We were friends and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: They trusted them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They trusted them, and they were good people. And they say, young people escape. When we leave to another city, the Nazis come in and the storm troopers come in and take over. And they're gonna kill you, and arrest you, and whatever. And we didn't believe it. We didn't believe it. One time the two Germans, the good Germans, asked my brother to make slippers. And he sent it to families in Germany and everything. And I was working with him. And he gave us a-- a pass, nobody should take us out for work. They're under his supervision. And I walked down for a minute. I walked down, and the German was talking to me. And I said, I don't understand so fast. He slapped me. He gave me-- he hit me. And the blood come out from my ears. And my brother saw this through the window. He told-- the-- the-- the German-- look, what they're doing to my brother. He just went out. He walked over to them, just leave him alone. He's under my protection. And I went back. And we decide we're not gonna stay here. We decide we're not going to stay here. And I went by myself to the other side, to Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I took a train from Warsaw to the border. It was nothing. And-- and I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when you went there, when you went there-- now you were going to leave, right? That was it. You didn't know when you were coming back. What did you bring with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just went there-- just I took-- I took my clothes, and everything. I went there to see what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take some pictures of something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't take anything. You were just going.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there. And later on I figured out here is-- everybody's free. You don't have to worry for the bombs and and everything. It was Communism. But we don't like them, anyway. But anyway it was free, people working in the streets, business. And I went back in the nighttime. I crossed the border. I had somebody. I was afraid to walk into the city. When I came to Warsaw, I was afraid to walk. People already had the-- the yellow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stars by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The people already had to separate-- who are Jews have to put to the-- the yellow star and everything. And I don't have a yellow star, and I was walking. And the tanks was going to the other front, the French-- it was the main highway, and I was walking at nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I have guts. And I walked over to a German. He was working with a-- it was an open car. And I said, can I take a lift? I was guts, to say-- but I didn't have a yellow-- he was thinking I'm a Polack. And he took me-- and I-- and I stopped from-- from my city about two kilometers. And-- and there lived my brother's in-laws. He married their daughter. They were very religious. And I walked into him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't want to go in at nighttime. The Polacks is gonna recognize me. And they're going to say, a Jew come back just now. And I went in, in the morning. And-- and I said so my father, pack. And let's go to the other side. And my father said, oh no. I don't want to go to the Russians. I remember the First World War, the Cossacks with the pogroms.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the problem. They remembered the Germans one way, and the Russians another way and they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- and-- he say, I would not go there. You know, the Germans-- they-- they don't do nothing to us. What they going to do? You want to go? And all five brothers went with me. We all went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you pack up? You packed up some things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we packed up the suitcases and everything. And we went, some went by bicycles. And some hitchhiked, we went to the border. We crossed the border, the German border. And I never forget. One German come over to me. He said, where you going? I said, my brothers are there today. I'm going to see my brother. And I had a bicycle with me. And I took off the chain. And I walked over to the German, you help me put the chain back? Just like this, and people say, you got guts. And he said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, to-- to divert his attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when I talk to him, he helped me. He was nice. And I said, where you going? I said, I'm going to see my brother. Auf wiedersehen. He said, you're not going to come back. Just like this. And we were there in-- in Bialystok, I would think. All of a sudden, we registered to go to the Black Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why all of a sudden? It wasn't-- you had a reason why you wanted to go to the Black Sea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were there. What we going to say in Bialystok, we intend to go, and I said in Russian was saved and everything. And it was an open registration. Where you want to go? And I registered. I'm going to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to go to Crimea.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Crimea, yes. But we went up, in the Ural Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, wait. Why did you choose the Crimea?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's warm. It's nothing-- I didn't want to go to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because Crimea is a seaside town.

speaker: interviewee
text: A seaside town.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to where-- to Odessa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was there in Crimea.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had been there before?

speaker: interviewee
text: After when I was coming home, out of Russia, I was never in Russia. I didn't speak Russian. But anyway, we were there-- in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: You figured you'd go to a nice, relaxing warm place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Little did you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later when we came to-- to the Ural Mountain, I said, I show you on the map, Magnitogorsk, the most gorgeous city.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where? Say the place again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Magnitogorsk. I show you on the map, Ural Mountains and Magnitogorsk. And we were there, my brothers, they took him to a-- they took-- they took my brothers to work, very hard work. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did? Now, how did you get to the-- you went to the Ural Mountains, and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we went to the Ural Mountains. We were all there. They give us quarters, where to live. And they go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, who? You registered with somebody? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you were in Bialystok. And you decided you wanted to go to Crimea. But you didn't get that far.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody stopped you on the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of to Crimea. The train went to Ural Mountain. We don't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got to Ural Mountain, they give us homes, where to live, family.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And you go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The trains were hijacked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, no, no. You go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, when you had-- you signed up to go the Crimea. You thought you would be working with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they fooled us. We-- they fooled us. Instead of going to Crimea, they went to-- to the Ural Mountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had registered to go to work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to-- to go to Crimea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was-- all of a sudden, we landed up in the Ural Mountain, in Magnitogorsk. It was a beautiful city, but it was Siberian. It was unbelievable to live there. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many brothers were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All four, All five.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all five together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- you had two brothers who were married already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three brothers married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were their wives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, with the wife. We brought the wives too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you weren't five people. You were eight people?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after we-- we-- we-- we-- when we were in Bialystok, we-- we have somebody to bring the wives there. And we were there already with the families. I was not married. We were there with the families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had children also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the children born already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes the children were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you were 10 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were five brothers with the wives, and then 11-- 12 people. My-- my younger brother, who you see there, he was married at 18. And he had a little boy. And my other brother who was in the picture, he had two girls, a boy and a girl. And my oldest brother had two girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. So there were five children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, five children. And we were there. They got a job. And I took a job to work in the steel. Like a mechanic, and I was working there, and I got better with them. They work hard-- later on, we got letters from Poland. Hitler did this. Send for your children and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And told them how you were fine here and everything. Why do they have to stay there, you know what I mean? And we got a letter from my father. Anybody who has the children there, has food and everything. They're working. It's pleasant. Come back. Now where was he still? Where was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was still in-- in Blonie--

speaker: interviewer
text: Still his town.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the little town. He said, come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that time, in truth, I think there was more food there than it was with the Russians. The Russians didn't have so much food.

speaker: interviewee
text: But wait a minute. He had no food. There was a ration. But the German told him how to send a letter to send to the people who ran away. Come back. Everything is fine here and everything. And we decide to go back. But we could not leave Magnitogorsk. Wherever people, you could get a ticket. But before the plane-- the train left, you know by train, police took you out. They went into this. And I got to know the chief in the police. And it's a small world. My brother married a girl in Samarkand. And the chief of the police was her cousin. Now later when we find out. And I made a deal with him. He was the chief.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Samarkand is Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It just was in-- in-- in Magnitogorsk. I made a deal with him to get me tickets. And I sold my suits and everything. There was nothing to buy. And I got a lot of rubles. And he helped me. And he stayed by the train. He was in charge of the train. And he said, don't look here. Here, everything is fine. And we got back to Bialystok. We came back to Bialystok. We decided to go back to Poland. My-- my brother who was in the picture, he went with his wife and the children. He was caught by the border, and his wife went through with the children. He was in Bialystok prison. I-- I know he was in prison. The other brother who died just now, his wife went with a little girl. She went. He-- he-- he could not go through. My older brother was afraid to go with his wife and children. And I decide. I'm by myself. I stayed with my brothers. They weren't there. We don't know what's happened. Later when they didn't come back, in Russia-- and-- and the Germans came back to Russian side. And registered, who wants to go back to Germany. This was in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German delegation come to the Russian side. They made up. Who wants to go back the Russians is gonna let them. They all registered to go back. Do you know what the Russians did? Everyone who registered to go back, they round them up and send them to prison. And I in the meantime was sick. I got appendix--

speaker: interviewer
text: Appendicitis.

speaker: interviewee
text: They rushed me to the hospital. And they operate on me, appendix busted. After I came out of the hospital, I went to Luck, a city there where I used to know people. And I worked in a-- in a store where they make ice cream. I eat a lot of ice cream. Later on I said, I come-- I come back to see my brother in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you did that, while you were in Luck, tell me what did-- do you remember the synagogue in Luck? The one that looked like a fortress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- Russia don't have synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was still standing. There's a famous one in Luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was standing, yes. I went back to Blonie. The synagogue was standing there too. Anyway-- but anyway, I went back there. And my brothers was already arrested. My brother who was in-- I mean and my older brother with his wife and children, his wife went through with a little girl. And I went to a-- a Communist. He was in Poland, a Communist. And he was exchanged for the Russian. In 1937, he was exchanged for the Russian people were [INAUDIBLE]. And he was a big Communist in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to him, and I said, what you want me to do? I want to do something. He said, look. I give you a place where to stay to hide. And I went there. And he went to the [NON-ENGLISH] over there, the secret police. He tell them, you want to find him? He is there. My friend. They came in, the Russian, and they arrest me. And they put me in Bialystok in prison. I was sitting in prison. I was tortured, tortured you'd never believe. They want to know why I register to go to Germany, you must be a spy. I said, a Jew is a spy? Day and night, they took me at 12 o'clock. We was in a cell, 40 people in a cell like this. And four beds-- everybody-- unbelievable situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me you are a spy and this. Later on--

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did they speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did they ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I asked for a Polish translator. I don't want it in Russian. I don't speak Russian. But anyway, later on they got me a Jew, a Russian Jew. He was worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Communist. The worst-- worst. And I said, I don't want you. Go to hell. If you want to kill me, kill me. Anyway, they tortured me. I was blue-- 12:00, 2 o'clock they took me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh I was there-- maybe for weeks they were questioning me. Later on, somebody came in to the-- our cell. And he said, you know your brother is next door in the cell? The brother who was at the wedding, his wife and-- they went through the border with the children, but he was arrested. And I said, my brother was arrested. And-- and-- and-- and I went out to the window. And I see they're walking around like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: The circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: To give them exercise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exercise in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to scream, Avraham! Avraham! And he recognized my voice, as my brother Abe, he was named. And he was stalling. He was going back and back to talk to me. And the guard, son of a gun, get out of there. We're gonna put you in solitary.

speaker: interviewer
text: To you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me. Don't talk to nobody. And the first thing he asked me, is my wife OK? I said, yes. She's on the other side with the children. We got news that she passed. And this way-- and he said to me, I'm-- I'm being sent away for five years. And I won't see you no more. A couple weeks later--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war, this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never saw him. After the war I met people who was with him in a concentration. And they say, everything he was there. And I-- I was looking on every train going to the army. He's there. And I didn't find him. But he was sent. When they came into me, I didn't see a trial. And they gave me five years hard slavery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slave labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's a concentration. I said, and the guard who gave the food, he said, you went to a trial? I said, no. They just gave me five years. They announced that they're gonna take me. He said, oh you. You're going to be forever. The people who get outside trial, they are [NON-ENGLISH], they call them. When your five years is over, they give another five, another five. You never get out. I said, that's good news.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was it? All you have done is you had-- you had signed up to go back to Germany. That was the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And they sent me there. And I was still not healed. I was still not healed. They put me on a train. You ever see 'Doctor Zhivago'? The train for the taking the-- the people like cattle into Siberia when they-- when the Russian took over, the-- the Communists took over. They sent the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did-- what kind of clothes? What did you have with you at the time when they took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When took me I have just a regular uniform, a-- a-- a prison uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a jacket, a sweater, nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And we were there. And we were in-- in-- in Novosibirsk, a prison, in Siberia. A couple-- couple months later on there was a rumor--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, from Bialystok, they took you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right to the-- right to the-- to the concentration.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put you on a train, and you went north?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a train like cattles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went north?

speaker: interviewee
text: North, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the Russians doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, with guards, with everything. And we got there, we worked maybe six, seven months. And I hear they're transferring us to another place, to the North Pole. I don't know where the North Pole. They took me to Krasnoyarsk. I show you here on the map. Krasnoyarsk is a city, the port city. From there, we-- we put us on a boat. I don't know what I-- a regular boat, but like cattle. And we were there for two, three weeks till we got to Igarka. There is nine months the dark. When we left, three months the sun, 24 hours the sun. Unbelievable the temperature, maybe miles, kilometers, ice. You cannot survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- what kind of drinking water? What-- how did you have? What did you drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they set-- they gave me water, even ice water. Who knows? It was water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you melt it down, and what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And-- and-- and-- in-- in the prison when we were going on the train, they stopped the train. And we-- we went out. And we eat snow, snow water. Who gave us this water. And we-- we-- we sucked the snow with-- with grease, with everything. But there, when we got to Igarka, I was working in the-- in the mountains with ice-- unbelievable. They took us out, and they were bagging lunch. I never forget. And I was running with a ladder. The ladder was not nailed. They were loose. And I fell down, and a-- a nail went down my hand, with-- with a head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you see in your hand today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, here. A nail went down. And I said to him, and I'm laying there bleeding. And the guards came in, and took me into the barracks. I came into the barracks was a prisoner, a woman, a doctor. This is unbelievable. And she said to me, she bandaged everything. And she put iodine. And she said, you're going to stay here for three days, not to go to work till that set. And I walked out from her. It's not a joke. I took off the bandage. And I put dirt in. I want to get poison. And three days later when I come back, everything healed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.
